quick_summary: Figma is the industry-standard browser-based design tool for UI/UX design, prototyping, and design systems. Understanding Figma is crucial for frontend developers who need to translate designs into pixel-perfect code and collaborate with design teams. Interview questions focus on component structure, auto-layout, and design handoff.

detailed_content.explanation:
Figma is a browser-based design tool for creating UI designs, prototypes, and design systems. The canvas is your infinite workspace where frames (artboards) represent screens or components. The Layers panel shows the document hierarchy, the Properties panel shows selected element properties, and the Assets panel holds reusable components.

Think of Figma's structure like HTML nesting: frames are like divs, they can contain other frames, text, and images. Every element has properties (position, size, color, effects) that you modify in the right sidebar. The key insight for developers is that Figma's layer hierarchy maps directly to your component tree — a frame inside a frame is a child component.
detailed_content.key_takeaway: Figma's auto-layout maps directly to CSS Flexbox — vertical auto-layout is `flex-direction: column`, horizontal is `row`, gap becomes the CSS `gap` property, and padding works identically.
detailed_content.try_it: Open any Figma community file, select a card component, and identify: the frame hierarchy, auto-layout direction, padding values, and corner radius. Translate those exact values into CSS.

pro_tip: In frontend interviews, mention that you use Figma's Inspect panel to extract exact CSS properties (colors, spacing, font sizes, border radius) — this shows you have real design-to-code workflow experience and can collaborate effectively with designers.
